questions.question: Jahangir's execution of Guru Arjan Dev was considered an unwise political decision.
questions.answer: Jahangir's execution of Guru Arjan Dev was considered an unwise political decision because it sowed the seeds of a long drawn struggle between the Mughals and the Sikhs. Guru Arjan Dev was the fifth Sikh Guru who had given shelter to Prince Khusrau at Taran Taran during his rebellion against Jahangir. While Jahangir punished him for supporting the rebel, this act created deep enmity between the Sikhs and the Mughal Empire. This hostility continued through Aurangzeb's reign, leading to the execution of Guru Tegh Bahadur and fierce conflicts with Guru Gobind Singh's Khalsa, all of which contributed to the weakening of Mughal power.

questions.question: Nur Jahan was able to wield great power during Jahangir's reign.
questions.answer: Nur Jahan was able to wield great power during Jahangir's reign primarily because of Jahangir's addiction to opium and alcohol, which kept him frequently absent from court. This made it easier for Nur Jahan to assert her authority and take decisions on matters of state. She was a woman of remarkable strength, courage, and shrewd planning. She often presided over the court alongside Jahangir and was seen in jharokhas receiving audience and issuing orders, sometimes without Jahangir being present. She was the only Mughal empress who had coins minted in her name, reflecting the extent of her influence and power.

questions.question: Shah Jahan punished the Portuguese at Hugli.
questions.answer: Shah Jahan punished the Portuguese at Hugli because they had been lifting orphaned children from Bengal and converting them to Christianity. The Portuguese had established a factory at Hugli and were misusing their presence by forcibly converting local children. This was highly objectionable to Shah Jahan as a devout Muslim. In 1631, he appointed Qasim Khan as the Governor of Bengal and gave him authority to deal with the Portuguese. The Portuguese were attacked and massacred, and Hugli was besieged for three months, effectively ending Portuguese power in Bengal.
questions.source_section: Shah Jahan - Policy Towards the Portuguese

questions.question: The Mughals repeatedly failed to hold Kandahar.
questions.answer: The Mughals repeatedly failed to hold Kandahar because it was a bone of contention between the Mughals and the Persians due to its strategic location on the north-western frontier. Shah Abbas of Persia first captured it in June 1622 during Jahangir's reign. Although Shah Jahan recovered it in 1638, the Shah of Iran captured it again in 1649. Three major military campaigns were launched to recover Kandahar but all failed. This demonstrated the declining military strength of the Mughal Empire and its inability to match the Persian forces at this crucial strategic point in Central Asia.

questions.question: Aurangzeb's religious policies led to the decline of the Mughal Empire.
questions.answer: Aurangzeb's religious policies led to the decline of the Mughal Empire because by imposing rigid measures, he turned almost every community against the Mughals. He reimposed the jizya and pilgrim tax on Hindus, losing their loyalty. He deprived Rajputs of higher posts and caused a rift in Mughal-Rajput relations by annexing Marwar. He destroyed Hindu temples like the Vishwanath temple at Banaras. He banned festivals, music, and jharoka darshan. He ordered the destruction of Sikh gurudwaras, leading to armed conflict with the Sikhs. These policies caused revolts by the Jats, Satnamis, Sikhs, Bundelas, Rajputs, and Marathas, all of which weakened and drained the empire.
questions.source_section: Aurangzeb as a Ruler / Reasons for Decline

questions.question: Shivaji was awarded the title of Chhatrapati.
questions.answer: Shivaji was awarded the title of Chhatrapati in 1674 when he crowned himself the Maratha king at Raigarh because of his extraordinary achievements in establishing an independent Maratha kingdom against all odds. He had captured the principality of Javli, killed Bijapur's general Afzal Khan, forced the Sultan of Bijapur to acknowledge him as an independent ruler, escaped from Mughal captivity at Agra, reconquered all the forts he had surrendered under the Treaty of Purandhar, and conquered territories in southern India including Jinji and Vellore. These remarkable military and political achievements earned him the right to crown himself as king.
questions.source_section: Rise of the Marathas and Chhatrapati Shivaji

questions.question: Aurangzeb's Deccan campaign contributed to the decline of the Mughal Empire.
questions.answer: Aurangzeb's Deccan campaign contributed to the decline of the Mughal Empire because he spent the last twenty-six years of his reign in the Deccan, which proved to be a devastating drain on the empire's resources. Although he annexed Bijapur in 1686 and Golconda in 1687, this brought the Mughals into direct conflict with the Marathas. The last twenty-five years were spent trying to suppress the Marathas, leading to a huge loss of soldiers, money, trade, and commerce. Most critically, his constant attention to the Deccan left the northern provinces unattended, allowing revolts to break out and regions to start asserting their independence.

questions.question: The reign of Shah Jahan is called the 'Golden Age' of the Mughal Empire.
questions.answer: The reign of Shah Jahan is called the 'Golden Age' of the Mughal Empire because during his 30-year rule, the empire was at its peak of prosperity and cultural achievement. The empire was politically united with no foreign invasions threatening its stability. The resulting peace, security, and stability led to remarkable developments in architecture. Shah Jahan was called the 'Prince among Builders' and built masterpieces like the Taj Mahal, the Red Fort, the Jama Masjid, and the city of Shahjahanabad. The empire was culturally and economically prosperous, making it a period of unmatched splendour in Mughal history.
